parts.heading: Introduction
parts.text: The As-Is Process area covers the current-state operation of Corporate Account Closure at Deutsche Bank — the controlled offboarding of a corporate client's banking relationship from the moment a closure instruction arrives to the final archiving of the account file. The process sits in the Client Lifecycle Operations domain and handles approximately 30 corporate closures per week. It is triggered by three distinct routes: a voluntary written instruction from an authorised client signatory, a bank-initiated decision following a commercial or risk review, and a dormancy review recommendation. Out of scope are credit facility closures (PRC-CR-0090), financial-crime exits (PRC-FC-0021), and retail customer closures. Understanding this process in its current state matters because account closure is a legally significant, often sensitive offboarding event: errors in authorisation, obligation clearance, or record-keeping carry regulatory and reputational consequences, and the quality of the client's final experience leaves a lasting impression of the bank.

parts.heading: Current state
parts.text:
The Roles & Organisation section documents six roles whose responsibilities and RACI assignments have been elicited and approved. The Closure Analyst (ROLE-CAC-002) is the process workhorse, holding the Responsible assignment across six of the eight steps — from logging the request, through mandate and obligations checks, to execution and archiving. The Relationship Manager (ROLE-CAC-001) is Accountable on the first and last steps and is kept informed throughout; this split ensures a client-facing owner without that owner running the operational controls. Finance (ROLE-CAC-005) and Compliance (ROLE-CAC-004) each hold a focused accountability: Finance owns residual balance disbursement at step 5 (PS-CAC-005), Compliance owns the combined sanctions and compliance screen at step 4 (PS-CAC-004). A dedicated Closure Approver (ROLE-CAC-003) provides the mandatory 4-eyes review at step 6 and must not be the same individual as the analyst who performed steps 2 through 5. Financial Crime (ROLE-CAC-006) enters only when a live sanctions hit is returned at step 4, receiving the case from exception EX-CAC-003 and owning resolution before closure can resume. The RACI matrix is complete and coherent across all eight steps.

The eight process steps form a tight linear sequence with well-defined branch points. Step 1 (PS-CAC-001) logs the request in the Client Lifecycle Workflow Tool and assigns one of four reason codes — CLIENT, BANK-COMMERCIAL, BANK-RISK, or DORMANCY — which gate the SLA target: BANK-RISK cases carry a 5-business-day cycle-time target, all others the standard 10. Step 2 (PS-CAC-002) verifies signatory authority against the account mandate in the Core Banking System, with an exception branch to EX-CAC-001 when authority cannot be confirmed. Step 3 (PS-CAC-003) performs the outstanding obligations check across the Core Banking System for transactions and balances, with Finance confirming open accounting items via the General Ledger through a workflow task with a 2-business-day turnaround; trade finance and FX positions are explicitly out of scope. Step 4 (PS-CAC-004) triggers an automated combined compliance and sanctions screen when the Closure Analyst advances the case in the workflow tool, with Compliance adjudicating the results; a sanctions hit routes to Financial Crime, an internal compliance case stays with Compliance, and either finding blocks closure via EX-CAC-003. Step 5 (PS-CAC-005) handles residual balance disbursement via the Payments Platform within 3 business days of compliance clearance; disbursements at or above EUR 100,000 equivalent require a callback confirmation before execution, though the currency basis of that threshold remains undefined (PG-CAC-001). Step 6 (PS-CAC-006) is the 4-eyes approval performed by the Closure Approver after reviewing the complete file. Step 7 (PS-CAC-007) executes the closure in the Core Banking System, setting all accounts to Closed and cancelling linked cards and payment instruments. Step 8 (PS-CAC-008) sends the closure confirmation to the client and archives the file in the Records Archive in accordance with the retention schedule, though neither the retention period nor the archive location is currently defined (PG-CAC-003).

The Exceptions section documents four off-path cases. Three carry MEDIUM impact: EX-CAC-001 (signatory authority not confirmed — SLA clock paused, case returned to the Relationship Manager), EX-CAC-002 (outstanding obligations remain — closure suspended, client notified to settle), and EX-CAC-004 (residual balance cannot be disbursed — balance moved to suspense as unclaimed funds). One carries HIGH impact: EX-CAC-003 (open compliance case or sanctions hit — closure fully blocked with an indeterminate timeline until Financial Crime or Compliance resolves the case). Frequency percentages are not documented for any of the four exceptions.

The Metrics section documents five measures. One has a measured actual: M-CAC-001 confirms process volume at approximately 30 closures per week. The remaining four — acknowledgement time (target: 1 business day), standard closure cycle time (target: 10 business days), exception resolution time (target: 5 business days from identification), and residual balance disbursement time (target: 3 business days from compliance clearance) — each have a target defined but no measured actual. The process is being managed against targets rather than against evidence of how it actually performs.

The Process Gaps section flags four open questions, each approved by the process owner. PG-CAC-001 asks whether the EUR 100,000 callback threshold at step 5 is a fixed EUR equivalent across all currencies or a per-currency nominal; without this clarification, the control may be applied inconsistently for non-EUR disbursements. PG-CAC-002 notes that the dormancy threshold triggering a closure recommendation at step 1 is not documented, leaving that trigger criterion without a defined parameter. PG-CAC-003 identifies that the record retention period and archive location at step 8 are unspecified, preventing the process from demonstrating compliance with record-keeping obligations. PG-CAC-004 records that no reversal procedure is defined for a closure executed in error at step 7. The Stakeholders section and the Pain Points section contain no elements and have not yet been visited.

parts.heading: What stands out
parts.text:
What is strong in this area is the coherence and completeness of the elements that have been documented. The eight process steps form an unambiguous linear flow, with every branch point leading to a named exception; the RACI matrix is full across all six roles and all eight steps; the 4-eyes separation between analyst and approver is explicitly enforced in both the role and the step elements. The compliance and sanctions design is notably well-constructed: a single automated combined query covers both sanctions and internal compliance cases in one advance of the workflow tool, with clear routing rules distinguishing Financial Crime (sanctions hits) from Compliance (internal cases). The process also demonstrates disciplined scoping, explicitly excluding credit facilities, FX and trade finance positions, and financial-crime exits, which limits the surface area to what is operationally manageable within this process.

What is thin or missing falls into three clusters. First, the metrics picture is a serious gap: four of the five documented measures carry no measured actual, meaning the bank is managing this process entirely against design targets with no empirical data on whether those targets are being met. Volume is known (30 per week), but cycle time, exception resolution, and disbursement performance are all unmeasured. Second, the Stakeholders and Pain Points sections are completely blank — no elements exist in either — leaving the as-is documentation without any account of who holds a stake in the process or how it feels to run it from the inside. Third, three of the four open process gaps carry operational or compliance risk that is unresolved: PG-CAC-003 (undefined retention period) is a record-keeping compliance exposure, PG-CAC-004 (no reversal procedure) leaves the team without recourse if a closure is executed in error, and PG-CAC-001 (undefined callback threshold currency basis) introduces inconsistency in a financial control. Exception frequency figures are also missing across all four exceptions, making it impossible to size the exception load or prioritise resolution efforts.

parts.heading: Recommendation
parts.text:
The most urgent action is to close the four open process gaps. PG-CAC-003 and PG-CAC-004 carry the highest risk: an undefined retention period is a demonstrable record-keeping compliance gap, and the absence of a closure reversal procedure means an erroneous execution has no defined remedy. Both should be resolved in a brief session with the process owner before this as-is documentation is used as a transformation baseline. PG-CAC-001 and PG-CAC-002 are design clarifications that affect consistency of execution and should follow immediately.

The second priority is to address the measurement gap. Operating at roughly 30 closures per week with no measured actuals on cycle time, exception resolution, or disbursement performance means the process owner has no evidence-based view of how the process actually performs against its SLAs. Establishing even basic timestamp capture at each workflow tool transition would convert four targets-only metrics into live performance indicators and provide the evidence base that transformation design will need.

Finally, the Stakeholders and Pain Points sections should be populated. Stakeholders are needed to identify governance ownership and regulatory sponsorship, and pain points are the primary internal signal that transformation design will draw on. With both sections blank, the as-is picture is incomplete as a foundation for improvement work.
